name: Elsevier Inc. v. Meta Platforms, Inc.

key_rulings.description: The court denied Dr. Saladin's motion to intervene without prejudice, holding that any renewed motion to intervene would be premature until a class certification schedule is set.
key_rulings.summary: The court construed a letter filed by Dr. Kenneth Saladin as a motion to intervene—either as of right or permissively—in which he sought to represent a proposed subclass with separate counsel. The court denied the motion without prejudice, allowing Dr. Saladin to renew it if and when the court sets a schedule for class certification briefing.

name: Encyclopaedia Britannica, Inc. v. OpenAI, Inc.

Plaintiffs' motion to compel further "Project Giraffe" discovery (ECF 1520) On Plaintiffs' three categories of information sought, I ordered the following: A. The parties are to meet and confer on the "five questions" about raw evaluation data; B. The clawed-back data will be logged and OpenAI will meet and confer on whether they can provide additional information requested concerning the clawed back document; and C. OpenAI is to log an evaluations that are withheld because they post-date the litigation. 2. News Plaintiffs' motion to amend the protective order (ECF 1518). Microsoft's motion for NYT Brand Tracker Survey data (ECF 1506) This motion is DENIED without prejudice. Parties are directed to continue meeting and conferring on production, including third-party Kantar in those efforts. 4. Microsoft Financials (ECF 1310) Parties are directed to cross-file supplemental briefs by Friday, May 15, 2026, with responses due Wednesday, May 20, 2026. Parties are to discuss why production of the financial information tied to LLM integration licenses is or is not a sufficient response to this request. The Clerk of Court is respectfully directed to close the following ECF Nos. in Case No. 25-MD-3143: 1506, 1513, 1518, 1520. SO ORDERED. (Signed by Magistrate Judge Ona T. Wang on 5/15/2026) Filed In Associated Cases: 1:25-md-03143-SHS-OTW et al. (ar) (Entered: 05/15/2026)

docket_entries.raw_text: JOINT STIPULATION AND ORDER STAYING ENCYCLOPAEDIA BRITANNICA ACTION: NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS HEREBY STIPULATED BY AND BETWEEN THE PARTIES THAT, subject to the Court's approval, as follows: 1. All proceedings and deadlines in the above-captioned Action are hereby stayed pending resolution of summary judgment motions in the other active cases in this multidistrict litigation. 2. Within 30 days of rulings on such motion(s) in the MDL, the parties shall file a joint status report in this Action to propose a case management conference and any appropriate further proceedings in light of the MDL decision. 3. While this stay is in effect, any party may move to lift the stay for good cause shown. Nothing in this stipulation shall preclude any party from seeking such relief, if necessary, or from seeking any other appropriate relief from the Court. SO ORDERED (Signed by Judge Sidney H. Stein on 4/21/2026) Case stayed. Filed In Associated Cases: 1:25-md-03143-SHS-OTW, 1:26-cv-02097-SHS (ks) Modified on 4/22/2026 (ks). (Entered: 04/21/2026)
docket_entries.summary: Judge Sidney H. Stein approved a joint stipulation submitted by both parties, ordering all proceedings and deadlines in this case stayed until summary judgment motions are resolved in the broader multidistrict litigation; within 30 days of those rulings, the parties must file a joint status report proposing next steps.

docket_entries.raw_text: OPINION AND ORDER RE: MONACO DEPOSITION: Mr. Monaco based his testimony-which should bind the corporation-on his own hazy recollections working on Project Giraffe, a review of source code that he and others wrote (but which he does not remember), and a few other documents: "part of" Lampe's deposition transcript, Lilian Weng's notes (which may or may not be the same as the Project Giraffe "meeting notes"), and "some metrics." (Jan. 27 Rough Tr. 19-21). He spoke to no other individuals to prepare for his deposition. But when asked even the simplest questions relevant to Plaintiffs' output claims, Mr. Monaco failed to answer or provided incomplete or evasive answers. For example, despite admitting that individuals other than he, Lampe, Weng and Goel and members of the legal team worked on Project Giraffe, he was unable to name a single other person who worked on Project Giraffe. Instead, he said he "would have to go back to" the meeting notes that he purportedly reviewed in preparation for his deposition to name a single other person who worked on the project. And then, when asked if he, Lampe, Goel and Weng performed "most" of the work on Project Giraffe, he paused for 18 seconds before stating that he was unable to answer the question. A 30(b)(6) witness cannot only rely on his own recollection if that recollection fails once he is asked anything more than the simplest, most basic questions. Mr. Monaco was not adequately prepared for his deposition. Mr. Monaco did not act alone. Mr. Monaco's answers became increasingly evasive after counsel's repeated and unnecessary objections, only a few of which were summarized here. At the March 10, 2026, conference, the Court directed the parties to meet and confer so that Plaintiffs can conduct a more fulsome deposition of OpenAI on the topics that Mr. Monaco was designated; the parties agreed to produce Mr. Monaco for an additional 3.5 hour deposition on April 8, 2026. OpenAI is on notice that Mr. Monacos preparation for his deposition in January was not sufficient, and that counsel's objections unnecessarily delayed and impeded the deposition. But, because Mr. Monaco will be produced for additional 30(b)(6) deposition time, which the Court expects will cure the deficiencies in the deposition from January, the Court defers ruling on whether any other remedial discovery or sanctions are warranted until after OpenAI's 30(b)(6) deposition(s) are completed. If sanctions are granted, such sanctions may be monetary, or they may include a recommendation that certain answers be imputed toor deemed admissions by-OpenAI. SO ORDERED. (Signed by Magistrate Judge Ona T. Wang on 4/7/2026) Filed In Associated Cases: 1:25-md-03143-SHS-OTW et al. (tg) (Entered: 04/07/2026)

docket_entries.raw_text: MINUTE entry before the Honorable Sunil R. Harjani: Before the Court is a motion to dismiss filed by Defendant Google, LLC. As is explained in the Court's standing order on motions to dismiss (available on the Court's website), the parties are advised that, when a motion to dismiss is filed, the nonmoving party has a right to amend its pleading once within 21 days. Fed. R. Civ. P. 15(a)(1)(B). Accordingly, consistent with the purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding," Fed. R. Civ. P. 1, plaintiff is directed to review Defendant Google, LLC.'s motion 21 to dismiss and to consider exercising, as appropriate, plaintiff's right to amend under Rule 15(a)(1)(B). If plaintiff elects to amend its pleading in response to the motion to dismiss, then defendant (unless otherwise ordered) must, within 21 days of the amended pleading, file either an answer or a renewed motion to dismiss. If plaintiff elects not to amend but instead chooses to litigate the motion to dismiss, plaintiff (unless ordered otherwise) must file its response within 21 days of the filing of the motion, and defendant must file its reply within 14 days of the filing of the response. By 6/23/2026, Plaintiff shall file a status report advising how it wishes to proceed. A telephone status hearing is set for 6/30/2026 at 9:15 a.m. The call-in number is (855) 244-8681 and the access code is 172 628 1276##. Attorneys of record may not use speakerphones during the status hearing. Members of the public and media will be able to call in to listen to this hearing but will be placed on mute. Persons granted remote access to proceedings are reminded of the general prohibition against photographing, recording, and rebroadcasting of court proceedings. Mailed notice (lxs, ) (Entered: 06/09/2026)
docket_entries.summary: The court issued a minute order advising plaintiff Kogon that, in response to Google's pending motion to dismiss, plaintiff has the right to amend the complaint once within 21 days under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 15(a)(1)(B), and directing plaintiff to file a status report by June 23, 2026, indicating how it wishes to proceed, with a telephone status hearing scheduled for June 30, 2026.

name: Amazon.com Services, LLC v. Perplexity AI, Inc.
court: Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit
court_full: Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

key_rulings.description: The court granted the motion to stay the district court's March 9, 2026 order pending appeal, applying the standard set out in Nken v. Holder, 556 U.S. 418, 434 (2009).
key_rulings.summary: A two-judge Ninth Circuit panel granted Perplexity AI's motion to stay the district court's March 9, 2026 order pending appeal, and the court expedited the appeal with briefing due between April and May 2026 and oral argument scheduled for June 2026.

key_rulings.description: The court granted an administrative stay of the district court's March 9, 2026 order, pausing its effect until the panel resolves the motion for a stay pending appeal.
key_rulings.summary: A two-judge Ninth Circuit panel temporarily stayed the district court's March 9, 2026 order while the court considers whether to issue a longer stay pending the outcome of the appeal.

docket_entries.raw_text: NOTICE OF ORAL ARGUMENT on Thursday, June 11, 2026 - 09:30 A.M. - SE 7th Flr Courtroom 2 - Scheduled Location: Seattle WA View the Oral Argument Calendar for your case here. NOTE: Although your case is currently scheduled for oral argument, the panel may decide to submit the case on the briefs instead. See Fed. R. App. P. 34. Absent further order of the court, if the court does determine that oral argument is required in this case, you are expected to appear in person at the Courthouse. If an in person appearance would pose a hardship, you must file a motion for permission to appear remotely by video, using ACMS filing type Motion to Appear Remotely for Oral Argument. Such a motion must be filed within 7 days of this notice, absent exigent circumstances. Please note that if you do file a motion to appear remotely, the court strongly prefers video over telephone appearance. Therefore, if you wish to appear remotely by telephone you will need to justify that request in your motion and receive explicit permission to do so. Be sure to review the GUIDELINES for important information about your hearing. If you are the specific attorney or self-represented party who will be arguing, use the ACKNOWLEDGMENT OF HEARING NOTICE filing type in ACMS no later than 28 days before the hearing date. No form or other attachment is required. If you will not be arguing, do not file an acknowledgment of hearing notice. [26-1444] [Entered: 04/09/2026 12:03 PM]
docket_entries.summary: The Ninth Circuit scheduled oral argument for June 11, 2026, at 9:30 a. m. in Seattle, though the panel may choose to decide the case on the written briefs alone without hearing argument. Attorneys who will argue must file an acknowledgment of the hearing notice at least 28 days before the date, and any party seeking to appear remotely must move for permission within 7 days of this notice.

docket_entries.raw_text: NOTICE OF ORAL ARGUMENT on Thursday, June 11, 2026 - 09:00 A.M. - SE 7th Flr Courtroom 2 - Scheduled Location: Seattle WA View the Oral Argument Calendar for your case here. NOTE: Although your case is currently scheduled for oral argument, the panel may decide to submit the case on the briefs instead. See Fed. R. App. P. 34. Absent further order of the court, if the court does determine that oral argument is required in this case, you are expected to appear in person at the Courthouse. If an in person appearance would pose a hardship, you must file a motion for permission to appear remotely by video, using ACMS filing type Motion to Appear Remotely for Oral Argument. Such a motion must be filed within 7 days of this notice, absent exigent circumstances. Please note that if you do file a motion to appear remotely, the court strongly prefers video over telephone appearance. Therefore, if you wish to appear remotely by telephone you will need to justify that request in your motion and receive explicit permission to do so. Be sure to review the GUIDELINES for important information about your hearing. If you are the specific attorney or self-represented party who will be arguing, use the ACKNOWLEDGMENT OF HEARING NOTICE filing type in ACMS no later than 28 days before the hearing date. name: Gracenote Media Services, LLC v. OpenAI Foundation

key_rulings.description: The court granted plaintiffs' motion to compel further 'Project Giraffe' discovery, requiring OpenAI to log withheld post-litigation evaluations and meet and confer on clawed-back documents, while denying other pending discovery motions without prejudice or as moot and ordering additional briefing on Microsoft financial disclosures.
key_rulings.summary: The court issued an order following a May 12, 2026 discovery status conference across several consolidated cases, granting one motion to compel related to 'Project Giraffe' evaluation data, denying two other motions to compel without prejudice, and directing the parties to meet and confer and file supplemental briefs on several remaining discovery disputes involving survey data, Microsoft financials, and a proposed protective order amendment.

key_rulings.description: The court ordered the case stayed pending resolution of summary judgment motions in the related multidistrict litigation, with either party retaining the right to move to lift the stay for good cause.
key_rulings.summary: The court approved a joint stipulation submitted by both parties, staying all proceedings and deadlines in this case until summary judgment motions in related cases within the broader multidistrict litigation are resolved. Judge Sidney H. Stein signed the order on April 21, 2026, requiring the parties to file a joint status report within 30 days of those rulings to propose next steps.

key_rulings.description: The court held that OpenAI's designated corporate witness was not adequately prepared for his 30(b)(6) deposition, ordered a follow-up deposition, and reserved the question of sanctions pending completion of that deposition.
key_rulings.summary: The court found that OpenAI's corporate representative, Mr. Monaco, was inadequately prepared for his Rule 30(b)(6) deposition and that defense counsel's repeated objections unnecessarily impeded the questioning; the court ordered OpenAI to produce Mr. Monaco for an additional 3. 5-hour deposition on April 8, 2026, and deferred ruling on sanctions—potentially monetary or adverse-inference admissions—until after that deposition is completed.

key_rulings.description: The court issued a show cause order requiring plaintiff to justify why the case should not be stayed pending summary judgment proceedings in the broader multidistrict litigation.
key_rulings.summary: The court ordered Gracenote Media Services to explain in writing by April 15, 2026, why this case should not be paused while summary judgment motions are resolved in the related multidistrict litigation cases; OpenAI may file a response by April 29, 2026.

docket_entries.raw_text: OPINION AND ORDER RE: MONACO DEPOSITION: Mr. Monaco based his testimony-which should bind the corporation-on his own hazy recollections working on Project Giraffe, a review of source code that he and others wrote (but which he does not remember), and a few other documents: "part of" Lampe's deposition transcript, Lilian Weng's notes (which may or may not be the same as the Project Giraffe "meeting notes"), and "some metrics." (Jan. 27 Rough Tr. 19-21). He spoke to no other individuals to prepare for his deposition. But when asked even the simplest questions relevant to Plaintiffs' output claims, Mr. Monaco failed to answer or provided incomplete or evasive answers. For example, despite admitting that individuals other than he, Lampe, Weng and Goel and members of the legal team worked on Project Giraffe, he was unable to name a single other person who worked on Project Giraffe. Instead, he said he "would have to go back to" the meeting notes that he purportedly reviewed in preparation for his deposition to name a single other person who worked on the project. And then, when asked if he, Lampe, Goel and Weng performed "most" of the work on Project Giraffe, he paused for 18 seconds before stating that he was unable to answer the question. A 30(b)(6) witness cannot only rely on his own recollection if that recollection fails once he is asked anything more than the simplest, most basic questions. Mr. Monaco was not adequately prepared for his deposition. Mr. Monaco did not act alone. Mr. Monaco's answers became increasingly evasive after counsel's repeated and unnecessary objections, only a few of which were summarized here. At the March 10, 2026, conference, the Court directed the parties to meet and confer so that Plaintiffs can conduct a more fulsome deposition of OpenAI on the topics that Mr. Monaco was designated; the parties agreed to produce Mr. Monaco for an additional 3.5 hour deposition on April 8, 2026. OpenAI is on notice that Mr. Monacos preparation for his deposition in January was not sufficient, and that counsel's objections unnecessarily delayed and impeded the deposition. But, because Mr. Monaco will be produced for additional 30(b)(6) deposition time, which the Court expects will cure the deficiencies in the deposition from January, the Court defers ruling on whether any other remedial discovery or sanctions are warranted until after OpenAI's 30(b)(6) deposition(s) are completed. If sanctions are granted, such sanctions may be monetary, or they may include a recommendation that certain answers be imputed toor deemed admissions by-OpenAI. SO ORDERED. (Signed by Magistrate Judge Ona T. Wang on 4/7/2026) Filed In Associated Cases: 1:25-md-03143-SHS-OTW et al. (tg) (Entered: 04/07/2026)
docket_entries.summary: The court found that OpenAI's corporate representative, Mr. Monaco, was inadequately prepared for his Rule 30(b)(6) deposition and that defense counsel's repeated objections unnecessarily impeded the questioning; the court ordered OpenAI to produce Mr. Monaco for an additional 3. 5-hour deposition on April 8, 2026, and deferred ruling on sanctions—potentially monetary or adverse-inference admissions—until after that deposition is completed.

name: Richner Communications, Inc. v. Microsoft Corporation

docket_entries.raw_text: CASE ACCEPTED AS RELATED. Create association to 1:25-md-03143-SHS-OTW. Notice of Assignment to follow. (vba)
docket_entries.summary: The court accepted this case as related to a multidistrict litigation proceeding (1:25-md-03143-SHS-OTW) and created a formal association between the cases, with a notice of assignment to follow.

docket_entries.raw_text: CONSOLIDATED MDL CASE: Create association to 1:25-md-03143-SHS-OTW. (vba)
docket_entries.summary: The court administratively linked this case to a consolidated multidistrict litigation proceeding, MDL No. 25-md-03143, in the Southern District of New York.

docket_entries.raw_text: CASE REFERRED to Judge Sidney H. Stein as possibly related to 1:25md3143. (sj)
docket_entries.summary: The court referred this case to Judge Sidney H. Stein as possibly related to multidistrict litigation docket 1:25md3143.

docket_entries.raw_text: CASE OPENING INITIAL ASSIGNMENT NOTICE: The above-entitled action is assigned to Judge Unassigned. (sj)
docket_entries.summary: The court's clerk issued an initial case assignment notice indicating that this newly filed action between Richner Communications, Inc. and Microsoft Corporation has been docketed in the Southern District of New York and is currently awaiting assignment to a presiding judge.

docket_entries.raw_text: Case Designated ECF. (sj)
docket_entries.summary: The court designated this case for Electronic Case Filing (ECF), meaning all future documents must be submitted through the court's electronic filing system.

docket_entries.raw_text: AO 121 FORM COPYRIGHT - CASE OPENING - SUBMITTED. In compliance with the provisions of 17 U.S.C. 508, the Register of Copyrights is hereby advised that a court action has been filed on the following copyright(s) in the U.S. District Court Southern District of New York. Form e-mailed to Register of Copyrights. (sj) (Entered: 06/25/2026)
docket_entries.summary: The court clerk submitted the required AO 121 copyright case-opening form to the U. S. Copyright Office, notifying the Register of Copyrights that a copyright action involving Richner Communications, Inc. and Microsoft Corporation has been filed in the Southern District of New York, as required by federal statute.
